Fatn Polrrrcal PnacrrcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
April 15,2004
Elizabeth WagnerHull
City of Chula Vista
Office of the City Attorney
2T6FourthAvenue
Chula Vista, CA 91910
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A4+052
Dear Ms. Hull:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Chula Vista City
Councilmanbers McCann, Rindone, and Salas for adviceregarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the..Act,).1
QUESTTON
May Councilmembers McCann, Rindone, and Salas participate in decisions to
transfer funds from the Merged Project Area or the Bayfront/Town Centre I project
Area?
CONCLUSION
It is presumed that the financial effect of these respective decisions on each
council member's real property is material. Therefore, each council mernber is
prohibited from participating in these decisions, unless this presumption is rebutted. In
order to rebut this presumption, each council member must show that it is not reasonably
foreseeable that a funding decision relating to a project within 500 feet of his or her
property will have any financial effect on the council member's property.
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl0l4. Commission regulations appearat Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
i@n


File No. A-04-052
Page No. 2
FACTS
The State of California's adopted budget for the current fiscal yem includes a shift
of redevelopment tax incremental funds from local redevelopment agencies statewide to
help offset current school revenue shortfalls. The Deparhnent of Finance has determined
the City of Chula Vista's obligation to be $401,768.7o fund this state Educational
Revenue Augmentation Fund ("ERAF') payment, the community development
departrnent is recommending that the agency appropriate $202,292 from the Merged
Project Area as a loan repayment to the Bayfront/Town Centre I Project Area. It is also
recommending the agency then appropriate$202,292 from the Bayfront/Town Centre I
Project Area and $199,476 from the Merged Project Area to fund the agency's ERAF
obligation.
The city council sits as the redevelopment agency. The City of Chula Vista is a
charter city governed by a council, consisting of four council members and a mayor,
elected from the crty at large. The city charter and agency rules provide for the approval
of resolutions by the affirmative votes of at least three members unless anotherprovision
of the charter requires otherwise. The charterprovides that appropriations made after the
adoption of the budget shall require the affirmative votes of at least fourmembers. To
process the appropriation of funds from one project area to another, and the subseQuent
appropriation to make the ERAF pa5rment, four of the five council/agency members will
need to participate in the votes.
Councilmember McCann owns 5 properties within the city and one of these, a
residential rental unit, is within 500 feet of the border of the Merged Project Area.
Councilmember Rindone owns one property, his primary residence, within 500 feet of the
Merged Project Area. Councilmember Salas owns two properties within the city and one
of these, herprimaryresidence, is located within 500 feet of the Bayfront/Town Centre I
Project Area. None of the properties within 500 feet of the project areas are actually
located within the redevelopment areas.
There are approximately 45,238 property owners in the City of Chula Vista.
Within the Bayfront/Town Centre I Project Area, there are approximately 409 property
owners, and within the Merged Project Area there are approximately 1,000 property
owners.
There are approximately 71,317 dwelling units within the city. Of those,
approximately 1,231 dwelling units are in the Bayfront/Town Centre Project Area and
306 are within the Merged Project Area.
ANALYSIS
The primary puqpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure
that "[p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, [should] perform their duties in an
impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests ofpersons who have supported thern." (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of


File No. A-04-052
Page No. 3
this goal, section 87 1 00 of the Act prohibits a public official from makin g, particrpating
in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under section 87100 requires
analysis of the following questions as outlined below.2
Questions one and Two: Are John McCann, Jerry Rindone, and Mary Salas each
considered a (public oflicial' and is each making particrpating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?
As members ofthe Chula Vista City Council, John McCann, Jerry Rindone, and
Mary Salas are each a "mernber, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agenct''and are, therefore, public officials subject to the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act. (Section 82048; regulation 1S701(a).)
A public official "makes a goveflrmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contracfual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (See regulation lB7OZ.l.)
Councilmanbers McCann, Rindong and Salas will "make a goverrmental
decision" if they vote on a decision to transfer funds from the Merged Project Area or the
Bayfront/Town Centre l. Additionally, any official who engages in any of the actions
described in enclosed regulations 187A2.2 and 18702.3 with regard to such a decision
will "participate in making" or "influence" that decision.
Question Three: What are the council members'respective economic interests -
the possible sources of a conflict of interest?
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision'1f it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
a
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investrnent 3 of $2,000 or more (section 82103(a);
regulation I 8703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partneE trustee,
2 These questions are based on the Act's conflict-of-interest analysis provided at regrlation I 8700(b).
3 An indirect invesknent or interest mearxi any investrrent or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by
a business entif or tust in which the official, the ofiicial's immediate family, or their agents own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a lO-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) 'Immediate family''is defined
at section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.


a
a
o
a
File No. A-O4-052
Page No. 4
employee, or holds any position of management (section 87103(d); regulation
18703.1O));
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103O); regulation
18703.2);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87 I 03 (c); regulation 187 03.3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87 1 03(e); regulation 187 03.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his orherpersonal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87103; regulation 18703.5).
Councilmembers McCann, Rindone, and Salas each have an Bconomic interest in
real property, assuming they each have an interest of $2,000 or more in each of their
respective properties.
In addition, because Councilmember McCann has an economic interest in any
person from whom he has received income of $500 ormore in the 12 months preceding
the decision, he has an economic interest in his renter if he has received payments
aggregating to this amount.
You have not provided information regarding any other economic interests that
Councilmembers McCann, Rindone or Salas mayhave. Forpurposes of this letter, we
assume that each has no other economic interests relevant to the decision you have
identified.
Question Four: Are the council members' economic interests directly or indirecfly
involved in the governmental deeision?
Real Property
Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a governmental decision if, among other things:
"(l) The real property in which the oflicial has an interest,
or any part of that real property, is located within 500 feet
of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the governmental
decision." (Regulation I 87 04.2(aX I ).)


File No. A-04-052
Page No. 5
Your question concerns decisions on funding for project areas within 500 feet of
property owned by Councilmembers McCann, Rindone, and Salas. Therefore, where the
property of a council member is within 500 feet of a particular project area, the council
member's property will be directly involved in a decision regarding that project area.
Consequently, the property of Councilmembers McCann and Rindone is each directly
involved in the finding decision relating to the Merged Project Area, while the property
of Councilmember Salas is directly involved in the decision relating to the
Bayfront/Town Centre I Pdect Area.a
Source of Income
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of
income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agencywhen that person,
either directly or by an agent:
(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official or
the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuancg renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject petrson."
(Regulation I 8704. 1 (a).)
Because the funding decisions were initiated by the Deparfrnent of Finance, a
source of income to Councilmember McCann such as his re,nter would be indirectly
involved in these decisions for purposes of the Act.
Question Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?
Real Property
If the real property in which an official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a governmental decision, the materiality standard of regulation 18705.2(a)
applies. (Regulation 187 04.2(cX I ).)
a In addition, the property of Councilmembers McCann and Rindone is also indirectly involved in
the funding decision relating to the Bayfront/Town Cente I Project Area, and the property of
Councilmember Salas is indirectly involved in the decision relating to the Merged Project Area. Where
real properly is not direcfly involved in a decision, the materiality standard for "indirectly involved" real
property provided at regulation 18705.2(b) (encloseQ is applied. (Regulation 1870a.2@)Q).)


File No. A-04-052
Page No. 6
Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on real property (other than a leasehold) which is directly involved in the
governmental decision is presumed to be material. "This presumption may be rebutted
by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any
financial effect on the real property." Qbid.) Please note that "any financial effect"
includes as little as a penny's worth.
Source of Income
In general for the decisions you have described, where an indirectly involved
source of income (e.9., a person renting Councilmember McCann's real property) is an
individual, the materiality standard of regulation I 8705.3 (b)(3) applies:
"The effect of a decision is material as to an individual
who is a source of income to an official if any ofthe
following applies:
(A) The decision will affect the individual's income,
investunents, or other tangible or intangible assets or
liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000 or more; or
(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property
interest in a manner that is considered material under Title
2, Califorrna Code of Regulations, sections 18705.2(b)."
(Regulation I 870s.3O)(3).)5
However, without further information regarding Councilmernber McCann's
renter, we carrnot conclusively identiff the applicable materiality standard.
Question Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the
goyernmental decision upon any of the eouncil members'economic interests will
meet the applicable materiality standard?
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it v/ill occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Ihorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Real Property
It is presumed that the financial effect of the respective decisions on each council
member's directly involved property is material. In order to rebut the presumption of
regulation 18705.2(a)(l), each council member must show that it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the funding decision relating to a project within 500 feet of his or her
5 Where the source of income is a business entity, applyregulation 18705.1(c), enclosed.
(Regulation 18705.3(bxl).) Where the source of income is a nonprofit entrty, apply 18705.3OX2),
enclosed.


File No. A-04-052
Page No. 7
property will have any financial effect on the council mernber's property. For example,
Councilmenrbers McCann and Rindone would each have to show that it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the decision to reduce funding by $202,292 and then $199,476 for the
Merged Project Area within 500 feet of each official's property will have any ftnancial
effect on the official's property. In comparison, Councilmember Salas would have to
show that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision to reduce funding by $202,292
for the Bayfront/Town Centre 1 Project Area within 500 feet of herproperty will have
any financral effect on herproperty.
Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In re
Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), the determination of whether or not it is reasonably
foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for the council members'
properties is necessarily a factual question that is ultimately for each council member to
decide. For purposes of this request, you have provided no facts rebutting this
presumption.
In addition, please note that for purposes of evaluating conflicts of interest under
the Act, governmental decisions are anallzod independantly to determine whether there
will be a foreseeable and material financial effect on a public official's economic interest.
(In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops.77.) Under certain circumstances, apublic official
disqualified from one decision may participate in other related decisions using the
Commission's "segmentation process," if the official's participation in the latter decision
does not result in a reopening of the decision from which the official was disqualified.
(See regulation 18709.) However, some decisions are too interrelated to be considered
separately because the official's conflict of interest in one decision will be disqualiffing
for the other. (Kilian Advice Letter, No. 4-89-522; Miller Advice Letter, No. A-82-119.)
For purposes of determining whether decisions can be segmented, decisions me
considered "'inextricably interrelated' when the result of one decision will effectively
determine, affirm, nullifu, or alter the result of another decision." (Regulation 18709(b).)
For example, in a decision to select one of two autopark sites, a decision to select one of
the sites is essentially a decision against the other autopark site. (Boogaard Adice
Letter, No.I-90-347.)
Based on the information you have provided, including the fact that Chula Vista's
ERAF obligation is set at $401,768, a decision to hansfer a certain amount of funding
from one project will effectively determine or alter a decision on the amount the city
should transfer from the other project. Therefore, the decisions to transfer funds from the
Merged Project Area and the Bayfront/Town Centre I Project Area are inexkicably
interrelated and cannot be segmented. As a result, Councilmembers McCann and
Rindone are also prohibited from participating in the decision to reduce funding for the
Bayfront/Town Centre 1 Project Area. In the same way, Councilmexnber Salas is also
prohibited from participating in the decision to reduce funding for the Merged Project
Area.
As a result, Councilmernbers McCann, Rindone, and Salas are prohibited from
participating in the funding decisions relating to both of these project areas.


File No. A-04-052
Page No. 8
Source of Income
Once Councilmember McCann has identified'the materiality standard applicable
to his renter, he must next assess whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a particular
project funding decision will result in a material effect on this source of income to him.
Because we do not have information regarding this source of incomg we are
unable to provide further analysis pertaining to any conflict of interest arising from this
economic interest. However, since CouncilmemberMcCann is already disqualified by
virtue of his real property interest, he cannot participate in the decisions.
Question Seven: The (Public Generalf Exception
Even if a public official determines that his or her economic interest will
experience a material financial effect as a result of the decision before the official, he or
she may still participate if the "public gener:allt'' exception applies to the economic
interest triggering the conflict of interest. (Regulation 187O7(b)(4).) However, the facts
you have presented do not suggest that this exception to the conflict-of-interest rules is
applicable to the situation of any of the council members.
Question Eight: "Legally Required Participation" Exception
Section 87101 permits an official who is otherwise disqualified from making a
govemmental decision to participate in the decision when the official's participation is
legally required. This would be the case if a quonrm could not be achieved without the
participation of disqualified public officials. The rule does not apply when there is an
alternative source of decisionmaking consistent with the statute authorizing the decision.
(Regulation 18708(a), copy enclosed.) Further, it does not apply when the disqualified
official's vote is merely needed to break a tie or when a quorum can be convened of other
members who are not disqualified. (Regulation 18708(c[1) - @.)
The "legally required participation" rule allows the participation of the smallest
number of disqualified persons necessary to constitute a quorum. (Regulation
18708(c)(3); In re Hudson (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 13.) A "quorum" is the minimum
number of members of a public agency required to conduct the agency's business, and
when the vote of a supermajority is required to adopt an item, the "quorum" is the
minimum number of members needed for that adoption. (Regulation 18708(d).)
The best random method of selecting which disqualified member should
participate is by lot. Other means of random selection that are impartial and equitable
may also be used. Whatever method is used, all disqualified officials must participate in
the random selection and all must be equally likely to be chosen. (Heisinger Advice
Letter, No. A-95-333.)


File No. A-04-052
Page No. 9
Once it is determined which disqualified official(s) will participate in a decision,
the ofEcial(s) will be selected for the duration of the proceedings in all related matters
unless one or more officials who were previously disqualified no longer has a financial
interest in the decision so that a quorum is then available.
A disqualified official who participates under the authority of section 87101 may
participate fully in the matter, including taking part in deliberations, voting in open
sessions of the body and in such closed sessions as is required by law. However, the
reinstated official may not attempt to influence the outcome of the matter "behind the
scenes" by engaging in private discussions with other memb€rs or staff. (Regulation
18708OX4); Grunwald Advice Letter, No. 4-95-184.)
You have stated that the City of Chula Vista's council consists of four council
members and the mayor and that, under the city charter and agelrcy rules, resolutions
must be approved by three or more members. Because Councilmembers McCann,
Rindone, and Salas are presumably disqualified from participating in these decisions, a
quorum would be lacking under the city's rules if approval of three or more mernbers is
required. In this situation, the "legally required participation" rule may be used to allow
the participation by one of the disqualified council memb€ns.
With regard to decisions for which approval of four or more members of the
council is required, the "legally required participation" rule maybe used to allow the
participation by two of the disqualified council manbers so that a quonrm of four (i.e.,
three council members and the rnayor) can be convened. Please note that all provisions
of regulation 18708 must be followed. (Regulation 18703.)
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
30
nocanegrf,
Counsel, Lngal Division

the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuancg renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject petrson."

a In addition, the property of Councilmembers McCann and Rindone is also indirectly involved in

real properly is not direcfly involved in a decision, the materiality standard for "indirectly involved" real

2, Califorrna Code of Regulations, sections 18705.2(b)."

renter, we carrnot conclusively identiff the applicable materiality standard.

Merged Project Area within 500 feet of each official's property will have any ftnancial
effect on the official's property. In comparison, Councilmember Salas would have to
show that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision to reduce funding by $202,292
for the Bayfront/Town Centre 1 Project Area within 500 feet of herproperty will have
any financral effect on herproperty.
Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In re
Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), the determination of whether or not it is reasonably
foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for the council members'
properties is necessarily a factual question that is ultimately for each council member to
decide. For purposes of this request, you have provided no facts rebutting this
presumption.
In addition, please note that for purposes of evaluating conflicts of interest under
the Act, governmental decisions are anallzod independantly to determine whether there
will be a foreseeable and material financial effect on a public official's economic interest.
(In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops.77.) Under certain circumstances, apublic official
disqualified from one decision may participate in other related decisions using the
Commission's "segmentation process," if the official's participation in the latter decision
does not result in a reopening of the decision from which the official was disqualified.
(See regulation 18709.) However, some decisions are too interrelated to be considered
separately because the official's conflict of interest in one decision will be disqualiffing
for the other. (Kilian Advice Letter, No. 4-89-522; Miller Advice Letter, No. A-82-119.)
For purposes of determining whether decisions can be segmented, decisions me
considered "'inextricably interrelated' when the result of one decision will effectively
determine, affirm, nullifu, or alter the result of another decision." (Regulation 18709(b).)
For example, in a decision to select one of two autopark sites, a decision to select one of
the sites is essentially a decision against the other autopark site. (Boogaard Adice
Letter, No.I-90-347.)
Based on the information you have provided, including the fact that Chula Vista's
ERAF obligation is set at $401,768, a decision to hansfer a certain amount of funding
from one project will effectively determine or alter a decision on the amount the city
should transfer from the other project. Therefore, the decisions to transfer funds from the
Merged Project Area and the Bayfront/Town Centre I Project Area are inexkicably
interrelated and cannot be segmented. As a result, Councilmembers McCann and
Rindone are also prohibited from participating in the decision to reduce funding for the
Bayfront/Town Centre 1 Project Area. In the same way, Councilmexnber Salas is also
prohibited from participating in the decision to reduce funding for the Merged Project
Area.
As a result, Councilmernbers McCann, Rindone, and Salas are prohibited from
participating in the funding decisions relating to both of these project areas.
Source of Income
Once Councilmember McCann has identified'the materiality standard applicable
to his renter, he must next assess whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a particular
project funding decision will result in a material effect on this source of income to him.
Because we do not have information regarding this source of incomg we are
unable to provide further analysis pertaining to any conflict of interest arising from this
economic interest. However, since CouncilmemberMcCann is already disqualified by
virtue of his real property interest, he cannot participate in the decisions.
Question Seven: The (Public Generalf Exception
Even if a public official determines that his or her economic interest will
experience a material financial effect as a result of the decision before the official, he or
she may still participate if the "public gener:allt'' exception applies to the economic
interest triggering the conflict of interest. (Regulation 187O7(b)(4).) However, the facts
you have presented do not suggest that this exception to the conflict-of-interest rules is
applicable to the situation of any of the council members.
Question Eight: "Legally Required Participation" Exception
Section 87101 permits an official who is otherwise disqualified from making a
govemmental decision to participate in the decision when the official's participation is
legally required. This would be the case if a quonrm could not be achieved without the
participation of disqualified public officials. The rule does not apply when there is an
alternative source of decisionmaking consistent with the statute authorizing the decision.
(Regulation 18708(a), copy enclosed.) Further, it does not apply when the disqualified
official's vote is merely needed to break a tie or when a quorum can be convened of other
members who are not disqualified. (Regulation 18708(c[1) - @.)
The "legally required participation" rule allows the participation of the smallest
number of disqualified persons necessary to constitute a quorum. (Regulation
18708(c)(3); In re Hudson (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 13.) A "quorum" is the minimum
number of members of a public agency required to conduct the agency's business, and
when the vote of a supermajority is required to adopt an item, the "quorum" is the
minimum number of members needed for that adoption. (Regulation 18708(d).)
The best random method of selecting which disqualified member should
participate is by lot. Other means of random selection that are impartial and equitable
may also be used. Whatever method is used, all disqualified officials must participate in
the random selection and all must be equally likely to be chosen. (Heisinger Advice
Letter, No. A-95-333.)
Once it is determined which disqualified official(s) will participate in a decision,
the ofEcial(s) will be selected for the duration of the proceedings in all related matters
unless one or more officials who were previously disqualified no longer has a financial
interest in the decision so that a quorum is then available.
A disqualified official who participates under the authority of section 87101 may
participate fully in the matter, including taking part in deliberations, voting in open
sessions of the body and in such closed sessions as is required by law. However, the
reinstated official may not attempt to influence the outcome of the matter "behind the
scenes" by engaging in private discussions with other memb€rs or staff. (Regulation
18708OX4); Grunwald Advice Letter, No. 4-95-184.)
You have stated that the City of Chula Vista's council consists of four council
members and the mayor and that, under the city charter and agelrcy rules, resolutions
must be approved by three or more members. Because Councilmembers McCann,
Rindone, and Salas are presumably disqualified from participating in these decisions, a
quorum would be lacking under the city's rules if approval of three or more mernbers is
required. In this situation, the "legally required participation" rule may be used to allow
the participation by one of the disqualified council memb€ns.
With regard to decisions for which approval of four or more members of the
council is required, the "legally required participation" rule maybe used to allow the
participation by two of the disqualified council manbers so that a quonrm of four (i.e.,
three council members and the rnayor) can be convened. Please note that all provisions
of regulation 18708 must be followed. (Regulation 18703.)
sections.conclusion:
It is presumed that the financial effect of these respective decisions on each
council member's real property is material. Therefore, each council mernber is
prohibited from participating in these decisions, unless this presumption is rebutted. In
order to rebut this presumption, each council member must show that it is not reasonably
foreseeable that a funding decision relating to a project within 500 feet of his or her
property will have any financial effect on the council member's property.

sections.facts:
The State of California's adopted budget for the current fiscal yem includes a shift
of redevelopment tax incremental funds from local redevelopment agencies statewide to
help offset current school revenue shortfalls. The Deparhnent of Finance has determined
the City of Chula Vista's obligation to be $401,768.7o fund this state Educational
Revenue Augmentation Fund ("ERAF') payment, the community development
departrnent is recommending that the agency appropriate $202,292 from the Merged
Project Area as a loan repayment to the Bayfront/Town Centre I Project Area. It is also
recommending the agency then appropriate$202,292 from the Bayfront/Town Centre I
Project Area and $199,476 from the Merged Project Area to fund the agency's ERAF
obligation.
The city council sits as the redevelopment agency. The City of Chula Vista is a
charter city governed by a council, consisting of four council members and a mayor,
elected from the crty at large. The city charter and agency rules provide for the approval
of resolutions by the affirmative votes of at least three members unless anotherprovision
of the charter requires otherwise. The charterprovides that appropriations made after the
adoption of the budget shall require the affirmative votes of at least fourmembers. To
process the appropriation of funds from one project area to another, and the subseQuent
appropriation to make the ERAF pa5rment, four of the five council/agency members will
need to participate in the votes.
Councilmember McCann owns 5 properties within the city and one of these, a
residential rental unit, is within 500 feet of the border of the Merged Project Area.
Councilmember Rindone owns one property, his primary residence, within 500 feet of the
Merged Project Area. Councilmember Salas owns two properties within the city and one
of these, herprimaryresidence, is located within 500 feet of the Bayfront/Town Centre I
Project Area. None of the properties within 500 feet of the project areas are actually
located within the redevelopment areas.
There are approximately 45,238 property owners in the City of Chula Vista.
Within the Bayfront/Town Centre I Project Area, there are approximately 409 property
owners, and within the Merged Project Area there are approximately 1,000 property
owners.
There are approximately 71,317 dwelling units within the city. Of those,
approximately 1,231 dwelling units are in the Bayfront/Town Centre Project Area and
306 are within the Merged Project Area.
sections.analysis:
The primary puqpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure
that "[p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, [should] perform their duties in an
impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests ofpersons who have supported thern." (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of
this goal, section 87 1 00 of the Act prohibits a public official from makin g, particrpating
in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under section 87100 requires
analysis of the following questions as outlined below.2
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; CONCLUSION appears before QUESTION

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: s one and Two: Are John McCann, Jerry Rindone, and Mary Salas each
considered a (public oflicial' and is each making particrpating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?
As members ofthe Chula Vista City Council, John McCann, Jerry Rindone, and
Mary Salas are each a "mernber, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agenct''and are, therefore, public officials subject to the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act. (Section 82048; regulation 1S701(a).)
A public official "makes a goveflrmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contracfual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (See regulation lB7OZ.l.)
Councilmanbers McCann, Rindong and Salas will "make a goverrmental
decision" if they vote on a decision to transfer funds from the Merged Project Area or the
Bayfront/Town Centre l. Additionally, any official who engages in any of the actions
described in enclosed regulations 187A2.2 and 18702.3 with regard to such a decision
will "participate in making" or "influence" that decision.
Question Three: What are the council members'respective economic interests -
the possible sources of a conflict of interest?
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision'1f it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
a
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investrnent 3 of $2,000 or more (section 82103(a);
regulation I 8703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partneE trustee,
2 These questions are based on the Act's conflict-of-interest analysis provided at regrlation I 8700(b).
3 An indirect invesknent or interest mearxi any investrrent or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by
a business entif or tust in which the official, the ofiicial's immediate family, or their agents own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a lO-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) 'Immediate family''is defined
at section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.


a
a
o
a
File No. A-O4-052
employee, or holds any position of management (section 87103(d); regulation
18703.1O));
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103O); regulation
18703.2);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87 I 03 (c); regulation 187 03.3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87 1 03(e); regulation 187 03.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his orherpersonal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87103; regulation 18703.5).
Councilmembers McCann, Rindone, and Salas each have an Bconomic interest in
real property, assuming they each have an interest of $2,000 or more in each of their
respective properties.
In addition, because Councilmember McCann has an economic interest in any
person from whom he has received income of $500 ormore in the 12 months preceding
the decision, he has an economic interest in his renter if he has received payments
aggregating to this amount.
You have not provided information regarding any other economic interests that
Councilmembers McCann, Rindone or Salas mayhave. Forpurposes of this letter, we
assume that each has no other economic interests relevant to the decision you have
identified.
Question Four: Are the council members' economic interests directly or indirecfly
involved in the governmental deeision?
Real Property
Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a governmental decision if, among other things:
"(l) The real property in which the oflicial has an interest,
or any part of that real property, is located within 500 feet
of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the governmental
decision." (Regulation I 87 04.2(aX I ).)
Your question concerns decisions on funding for project areas within 500 feet of
property owned by Councilmembers McCann, Rindone, and Salas. Therefore, where the
property of a council member is within 500 feet of a particular project area, the council
member's property will be directly involved in a decision regarding that project area.
Consequently, the property of Councilmembers McCann and Rindone is each directly
involved in the finding decision relating to the Merged Project Area, while the property
of Councilmember Salas is directly involved in the decision relating to the
Bayfront/Town Centre I Pdect Area.a
Source of Income
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of
income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agencywhen that person,
either directly or by an agent:
(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official or
the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuancg renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject petrson."
(Regulation I 8704. 1 (a).)
Because the funding decisions were initiated by the Deparfrnent of Finance, a
source of income to Councilmember McCann such as his re,nter would be indirectly
involved in these decisions for purposes of the Act.
Question Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?
Real Property
If the real property in which an official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a governmental decision, the materiality standard of regulation 18705.2(a)
applies. (Regulation 187 04.2(cX I ).)
a In addition, the property of Councilmembers McCann and Rindone is also indirectly involved in
the funding decision relating to the Bayfront/Town Cente I Project Area, and the property of
Councilmember Salas is indirectly involved in the decision relating to the Merged Project Area. Where
real properly is not direcfly involved in a decision, the materiality standard for "indirectly involved" real
property provided at regulation 18705.2(b) (encloseQ is applied. (Regulation 1870a.2@)Q).)
Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on real property (other than a leasehold) which is directly involved in the
governmental decision is presumed to be material. "This presumption may be rebutted
by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any
financial effect on the real property." Qbid.) Please note that "any financial effect"
includes as little as a penny's worth.
Source of Income
In general for the decisions you have described, where an indirectly involved
source of income (e.9., a person renting Councilmember McCann's real property) is an
individual, the materiality standard of regulation I 8705.3 (b)(3) applies:
"The effect of a decision is material as to an individual
who is a source of income to an official if any ofthe
following applies:
(A) The decision will affect the individual's income,
investunents, or other tangible or intangible assets or
liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000 or more; or
(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property
interest in a manner that is considered material under Title
2, Califorrna Code of Regulations, sections 18705.2(b)."
(Regulation I 870s.3O)(3).)5
However, without further information regarding Councilmernber McCann's
renter, we carrnot conclusively identiff the applicable materiality standard.
Question Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the
goyernmental decision upon any of the eouncil members'economic interests will
meet the applicable materiality standard?
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it v/ill occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Ihorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Real Property
It is presumed that the financial effect of the respective decisions on each council
member's directly involved property is material. In order to rebut the presumption of
regulation 18705.2(a)(l), each council member must show that it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the funding decision relating to a project within 500 feet of his or her
5 Where the source of income is a business entity, applyregulation 18705.1(c), enclosed.
(Regulation 18705.3(bxl).) Where the source of income is a nonprofit entrty, apply 18705.3OX2),
enclosed.
property will have any financial effect on the council mernber's property. For example,
Councilmenrbers McCann and Rindone would each have to show that it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the decision to reduce funding by $202,292 and then $199,476 for the
Merged Project Area within 500 feet of each official's property will have any ftnancial
effect on the official's property. In comparison, Councilmember Salas would have to
show that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision to reduce funding by $202,292
for the Bayfront/Town Centre 1 Project Area within 500 feet of herproperty will have
any financral effect on herproperty.
Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In re
Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), the determination of whether or not it is reasonably
foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for the council members'
properties is necessarily a factual question that is ultimately for each council member to
decide. For purposes of this request, you have provided no facts rebutting this
presumption.
In addition, please note that for purposes of evaluating conflicts of interest under
the Act, governmental decisions are anallzod independantly to determine whether there
will be a foreseeable and material financial effect on a public official's economic interest.
(In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops.77.) Under certain circumstances, apublic official
disqualified from one decision may participate in other related decisions using the
Commission's "segmentation process," if the official's participation in the latter decision
does not result in a reopening of the decision from which the official was disqualified.
(See regulation 18709.) However, some decisions are too interrelated to be considered
separately because the official's conflict of interest in one decision will be disqualiffing
for the other. (Kilian Advice Letter, No. 4-89-522; Miller Advice Letter, No. A-82-119.)
For purposes of determining whether decisions can be segmented, decisions me
considered "'inextricably interrelated' when the result of one decision will effectively
determine, affirm, nullifu, or alter the result of another decision." (Regulation 18709(b).)
For example, in a decision to select one of two autopark sites, a decision to select one of
the sites is essentially a decision against the other autopark site. (Boogaard Adice
Letter, No.I-90-347.)
Based on the information you have provided, including the fact that Chula Vista's
ERAF obligation is set at $401,768, a decision to hansfer a certain amount of funding
from one project will effectively determine or alter a decision on the amount the city
should transfer from the other project. Therefore, the decisions to transfer funds from the
Merged Project Area and the Bayfront/Town Centre I Project Area are inexkicably
interrelated and cannot be segmented. As a result, Councilmembers McCann and
Rindone are also prohibited from participating in the decision to reduce funding for the
Bayfront/Town Centre 1 Project Area. In the same way, Councilmexnber Salas is also
prohibited from participating in the decision to reduce funding for the Merged Project
Area.
As a result, Councilmernbers McCann, Rindone, and Salas are prohibited from
participating in the funding decisions relating to both of these project areas.
Source of Income
Once Councilmember McCann has identified'the materiality standard applicable
to his renter, he must next assess whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a particular
project funding decision will result in a material effect on this source of income to him.
Because we do not have information regarding this source of incomg we are
unable to provide further analysis pertaining to any conflict of interest arising from this
economic interest. However, since CouncilmemberMcCann is already disqualified by
virtue of his real property interest, he cannot participate in the decisions.
Question Seven: The (Public Generalf Exception
Even if a public official determines that his or her economic interest will
experience a material financial effect as a result of the decision before the official, he or
she may still participate if the "public gener:allt'' exception applies to the economic
interest triggering the conflict of interest. (Regulation 187O7(b)(4).) However, the facts
you have presented do not suggest that this exception to the conflict-of-interest rules is
applicable to the situation of any of the council members.
Question Eight: "Legally Required Participation" Exception
Section 87101 permits an official who is otherwise disqualified from making a
govemmental decision to participate in the decision when the official's participation is
legally required. This would be the case if a quonrm could not be achieved without the
participation of disqualified public officials. The rule does not apply when there is an
alternative source of decisionmaking consistent with the statute authorizing the decision.
(Regulation 18708(a), copy enclosed.) Further, it does not apply when the disqualified
official's vote is merely needed to break a tie or when a quorum can be convened of other
members who are not disqualified. (Regulation 18708(c[1) - @.)
The "legally required participation" rule allows the participation of the smallest
number of disqualified persons necessary to constitute a quorum. (Regulation
18708(c)(3); In re Hudson (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 13.) A "quorum" is the minimum
number of members of a public agency required to conduct the agency's business, and
when the vote of a supermajority is required to adopt an item, the "quorum" is the
minimum number of members needed for that adoption. (Regulation 18708(d).)
The best random method of selecting which disqualified member should
participate is by lot. Other means of random selection that are impartial and equitable
may also be used. Whatever method is used, all disqualified officials must participate in
the random selection and all must be equally likely to be chosen. (Heisinger Advice
Letter, No. A-95-333.)
Once it is determined which disqualified official(s) will participate in a decision,
the ofEcial(s) will be selected for the duration of the proceedings in all related matters
unless one or more officials who were previously disqualified no longer has a financial
interest in the decision so that a quorum is then available.
A disqualified official who participates under the authority of section 87101 may
participate fully in the matter, including taking part in deliberations, voting in open
sessions of the body and in such closed sessions as is required by law. However, the
reinstated official may not attempt to influence the outcome of the matter "behind the
scenes" by engaging in private discussions with other memb€rs or staff. (Regulation
18708OX4); Grunwald Advice Letter, No. 4-95-184.)
You have stated that the City of Chula Vista's council consists of four council
members and the mayor and that, under the city charter and agelrcy rules, resolutions
must be approved by three or more members. Because Councilmembers McCann,
Rindone, and Salas are presumably disqualified from participating in these decisions, a
quorum would be lacking under the city's rules if approval of three or more mernbers is
required. In this situation, the "legally required participation" rule may be used to allow
the participation by one of the disqualified council memb€ns.
With regard to decisions for which approval of four or more members of the
council is required, the "legally required participation" rule maybe used to allow the
participation by two of the disqualified council manbers so that a quonrm of four (i.e.,
three council members and the rnayor) can be convened. Please note that all provisions
of regulation 18708 must be followed. (Regulation 18703.)

CONCLUSION: It is presumed that the financial effect of these respective decisions on each
council member's real property is material. Therefore, each council mernber is
prohibited from participating in these decisions, unless this presumption is rebutted. In
order to rebut this presumption, each council member must show that it is not reasonably
foreseeable that a funding decision relating to a project within 500 feet of his or her
property will have any financial effect on the council member's property.

The charterprovides that appropriations made after the adoption of the budget shall require the affirmative votes of at least fourmembers. To process the appropriation of funds from one project area to another, and the subseQuent appropriation to make the ERAF pa5rment, four of the five council/agency members will need to participate in the votes. Councilmember McCann owns 5 properties within the city and one of these, a residential rental unit, is within 500 feet of the border of the Merged Project Area. Councilmember Rindone owns one property, his primary residence, within 500 feet of the Merged Project Area.
